content.value: The Docker Command Line Interface (CLI) is the primary way developers interact with Docker. These commands allow you to manage the entire lifecycle of images and containers. Below, we have categorized the most essential commands you need to master for the VTU curriculum.
Use these commands to verify your Docker installation and system state.

contentBlocks.value: Usage: Checks the installed Docker version on your system.
docker --version
contentBlocks.label: Terminal

contentBlocks.value: Tip: Use docker version (without dashes) to see detailed information about both the client and server components.

content.value: These commands are used to manage Docker Images, which are the blueprints for your containers.

contentBlocks.value: Usage: Downloads a specific image from a registry (usually Docker Hub) to your local machine.
docker pull <image_name>
contentBlocks.label: Example: docker pull nginx

contentBlocks.value: Usage: Lists all the Docker images currently stored on your local system.
docker images
contentBlocks.label: Terminal

content.value: These commands manage the active instances of your images. Click each command to see its specific feature.

contentBlocks.value: Usage: Creates and starts a new container from an image. If the image isn't local, it pulls it first.
docker run -d --name my-container nginx
contentBlocks.label: Example

contentBlocks.value: Usage: Lists running containers. Use -a to see all containers (including stopped ones).
docker ps -a
contentBlocks.label: Terminal

contentBlocks.value: Usage: Runs a new command inside an already running container. Commonly used to open a shell.
docker exec -it <container_id> bash
contentBlocks.label: Example

contentBlocks.value: docker stop: Gracefully shuts down a container by sending a SIGTERM signal.
docker kill: Forcefully stops a container by sending a SIGKILL signal.

docker stop <container_name>
docker kill <container_name>
contentBlocks.label: Terminal

contentBlocks.value: docker pause: Suspends all processes in a container.
docker unpause: Resumes all processes in a previously paused container.
Usage: Restarts a container that is either running or stopped.
Usage: Removes one or more stopped containers from the system.
docker rm <container_id>
contentBlocks.label: Terminal

content.value: The diagram and interactive simulation below show how these commands move a container through its different states.

content.caption: The complete Docker Container Lifecycle states.
